rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical (3+2) cyclization between amides and olefins using DCE as a chlorine source to give 2-oxazolines via chloronium-ion intermediates
electrochemical_role: Consecutive paired electrolysis: DCE is reduced at the cathode to release chloride (with H2 evolution), then chloride is oxidized at the anode to a chlorine radical/Cl+ equivalent that forms a chloronium ion with the alkene; the chloride anion functions as a redox mediator generated in situ

key_intermediates.name: chloride anion (from cathodic reduction of DCE)
chlorine radical / Cl+ (anodic oxidation of Cl-)
chloronium ion of olefin (3-membered cyclic chloriranium)

electron_flow: At the cathode, DCE accepts 2 e- to release 2 Cl- (and ethylene/H2 byproducts); at the anode, Cl- is oxidized (-2e-) to Cl+/Cl•, which adds to the olefin to form a chloronium intermediate that is opened by the amide oxygen, followed by deprotonation and cyclization (intramolecular N attack with loss of HCl) to give the oxazoline.
rate_determining_step: Electrochemical redox step (first-order in current; zero-order in both indene and benzamide), consistent with anodic Cl- oxidation being rate-limiting

probe_reagents.name: AgNO3 (chloride detection)
probe_reagents.observation: White AgCl precipitate formed from electrolyzed solution, confirming Cl- generation; solution pH = 3.19 (acidic)

probe_reagents.name: Styrene (as model olefin without base)
probe_reagents.observation: Isolated β-chloroamides 5bc (40%) and 5bc' (41%); subsequent treatment with NaOAc converted them to oxazolines 4bc/4bc' (35%/33%), supporting them as on-cycle intermediates

probe_reagents.name: HCl additive (10 mol%) in THF
probe_reagents.observation: In THF (no DCE chloride source) yield dropped to 11%; adding 10 mol% HCl restored 51% yield with r.r. = 10:1, confirming the essential role of chloride

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of DCE, 1a, 2a, TBACl
probe_reagents.observation: DCE reduces at -1.5 V vs Ag/AgCl; Cl- oxidation at 1.5 V is lower than 1a (1.8 V) or 2a, so Cl- is preferentially oxidized at the anode

notes: Authors note an induction period in time-monitored conversion (consistent with Cl- buildup before productive turnover). An alternative pathway in which the indene is first anodically oxidized to a radical cation cannot be excluded (see SI Figure S9); the favored pathway proceeds via chloronium ion formed from anodically generated Cl+/Cl•.
